title: Enums & Records
subtitle: Give your data tidy name tags and build mini data classes in one short line.
goal: Use an enum to hold a fixed set of named choices (and switch on them), and use a record to make a short, read-only data class with free getters, equals, and toString.

blocks.heading: Why enums? A fixed set of choices 🏷️
blocks.html:
Some things in life have a fixed set of options. A traffic light is RED, YELLOW, or GREEN — never "purple". The days of the week are MON through SUN — never "Funday".

An enum (short for enumeration) lets you give Java that exact list of allowed names. Once you do, Java won't let anyone sneak in a value that isn't on the list. No typos, no surprises.

blocks.caption: An enum is just a list of named constants
blocks.explain: enum TrafficLight { RED, YELLOW, GREEN } says "the only valid lights are these three." Each name is a constant you reach with TrafficLight.RED. A switch can branch on every option, and the arrow -> form is short and tidy.

blocks.html: Enum names are SHOUTY. By tradition we write enum constants in UPPER_CASE like RED and GREEN. It makes them easy to spot as fixed values.

blocks.heading: Enums can carry extra info 🎴
blocks.html:
An enum constant can be more than a name — it can hold its own little piece of data. Here each card suit also remembers the symbol that's printed on the card.

You add a field, a tiny constructor, and a method to read it. Then you can loop over every option with values().

blocks.caption: Each suit knows its own symbol
blocks.output:
HEARTS looks like ♥
DIAMONDS looks like ♦
CLUBS looks like ♣
SPADES looks like ♠
blocks.explain: HEARTS("♥") passes the symbol into the constructor, which saves it in the symbol field. values() gives you every constant in order, so the loop visits all four suits. Notice the field is final — once set, a suit's symbol never changes.

blocks.question: What is an enum best used for?
options: Storing a number that changes a lot
A fixed set of named choices, like RED, YELLOW, GREEN
Reading text typed by the user
blocks.explain: An enum locks in a fixed list of allowed values. Java then refuses anything not on that list, which stops typos and invalid states.

blocks.heading: Records: a data class in one line 📦
blocks.html:
Lots of the time you just want a little bag of data — a Point with an x and a y, or a Person with a name and an age. The old way meant writing a class with fields, a constructor, getters, equals, and toString by hand. Tedious!

A record (Java 16+) writes all of that for you. You declare the pieces of data, and Java fills in the rest.

blocks.caption: One short line gives you a full data class
blocks.output:
Point[x=1, y=2]
x is 1, y is 2
Person[name=Alice, age=30]
Same person? true
blocks.explain: record Point(int x, int y) {} hands you a constructor (new Point(1, 2)), getters named x() and y(), a neat toString that prints Point[x=1, y=2], and an equals that says two records are equal when all their parts match — which is why alice.equals(alice2) is true. Records are immutable: once built, their values can't change.

blocks.html: Record getters have no "get". You read a record's value with p.x(), not p.getX(). The method is named after the field itself.

blocks.heading: Record or regular class? 🤔
blocks.html:
Reach for a record when the object is mostly just data that never changes — coordinates, a person's name and age, a setting. You get clean code for free.

Reach for a regular class when the object needs to change over time, hide its insides, or do a lot of behavior. Records are locked and read-only on purpose, so they aren't the right tool when you need mutation.

blocks.question: What does a record give you automatically?
options: A getter, equals, and a readable toString
A way to change its fields later
Nothing — you write it all yourself
blocks.explain: A record auto-generates the constructor, getters (named like the fields), equals, hashCode, and toString. Its data is immutable, so it does NOT give you a way to change fields later.

blocks.question: How does a record's toString print a Point(1, 2)?
blocks.explain: A record's built-in toString uses the format Name[field=value, field=value], so a Point with x=1 and y=2 prints exactly Point[x=1, y=2].

blocks.title: Make your own enum
blocks.instructions: Inside Main, create an enum called Pet with three constants: DOG, CAT, and FISH. The loop already prints every value — just fill in the enum line so it compiles and runs.
blocks.solutionOutput:
Pet: DOG
Pet: CAT
Pet: FISH
blocks.passMsg: Nice! You listed a fixed set of choices and looped over every one. 🐾
blocks.failMsg: Put the three names inside the braces: enum Pet { DOG, CAT, FISH }

blocks.title: Build a record
blocks.instructions: Declare a record named Animal with a String name and an int age. The main method already builds one and prints it — just add the record line at the bottom so it compiles.
blocks.solutionOutput:
Animal[name=Rex, age=5]
Rex is 5 years old
blocks.passMsg: One line, a whole data class! Look at that free toString. 📦
blocks.failMsg: Add this at the bottom: record Animal(String name, int age) {}

blocks.title: Surprise Quiz! Days 16–20
blocks.intro: Pop quiz! Let's see what stuck from the last 5 days. No grades, just fun 🎉

questions.question: Which pair of keywords lets you try risky code and handle an error if it happens?
questions.explain: try runs the risky code; catch handles the exception if one is thrown, so the program doesn't crash. (Day 16!)

questions.question: What is special about an ArrayList compared to a plain array?
options: It can grow and shrink as you add or remove items
It is always exactly 10 items long
questions.explain: An ArrayList resizes itself, so you can add() and remove() items freely. A plain array has a fixed length. (Day 17!)

questions.question: Which collection stores key-value pairs, like a name and a score?
questions.explain: A HashMap links each key to a value: map.put("Sam", 10) then map.get("Sam") gives back 10. (Day 18!)

questions.question: What does the T in a generic class like Box<T> stand for?
options: A fixed type that is always String
A type placeholder you fill in when you use the class
questions.explain: T is a type parameter, a placeholder. Box<String> makes a Box that holds Strings; Box<Integer> holds Integers. (Day 19!)

questions.question: When should you use an enum?
options: To store a fixed set of named choices, like days of the week
To make a loop run forever
questions.explain: An enum is perfect for a fixed list of named values, like Color.RED, Color.GREEN, Color.BLUE. (Day 20!)

blocks.scenario: Scenario: You manage a small inventory. Each product has a name, category (enum), and price (record). List all products, then filter only the ones under ₹500 and print a summary.
blocks.caption: Inventory report with enum + record
blocks.output:
=== Full Inventory ===
Headphones [ELECTRONICS] - Rs.1200.0
Rice 5kg [FOOD] - Rs.320.0
T-Shirt [CLOTHING] - Rs.450.0
USB Cable [ELECTRONICS] - Rs.150.0

=== Under Rs.500 ===
- Rice 5kg (Rs.320.0)
- T-Shirt (Rs.450.0)
- USB Cable (Rs.150.0)
3 affordable item(s)
blocks.explain: This uses an enum for fixed categories (Day 20), a record for immutable product data (Day 20), an ArrayList to hold the inventory (Day 17), and a for-each loop with an if filter (Days 6, 8).

blocks.title: Milestone Exam — Days 16–20
blocks.intro: Exceptions, collections, generics, and modern Java! 70% to pass.

questions.question: Which pair of keywords handles risky code and catches errors?
questions.explain: try runs code that might fail; catch handles the exception so the program doesn’t crash. (Day 16)

questions.question: What makes ArrayList different from a plain array?
options: ArrayList can grow and shrink dynamically
ArrayList cannot use a for-each loop
questions.explain: An ArrayList resizes automatically when you add() or remove() items. A plain array has a fixed length. (Day 17)

questions.question: Which collection stores key-value pairs?
questions.explain: HashMap maps each key to a value, like map.put("name", "Sam") and map.get("name") returns "Sam". (Day 18)

questions.question: What does T in Box<T> represent?
options: A type placeholder you fill in when using the class
questions.explain: T is a generic type parameter. Box<String> makes a Box holding Strings; Box<Integer> holds Integers. (Day 19)

questions.question: What does a record give you for free?
options: A way to change its fields later
questions.explain: A record auto-generates constructor, getters (named like fields), equals, hashCode, and toString. Data is immutable. (Day 20)

blocks.scenario: Scenario: You manage a small inventory. Each product has a name, category (enum), and price (record). List all products, then filter only the ones under ₹500.
blocks.caption: Inventory report with enum + record
blocks.output:
=== Full Inventory ===
Headphones [ELECTRONICS] - Rs.1200.0
Rice 5kg [FOOD] - Rs.320.0
T-Shirt [CLOTHING] - Rs.450.0
USB Cable [ELECTRONICS] - Rs.150.0

=== Under Rs.500 ===
- Rice 5kg (Rs.320.0)
- T-Shirt (Rs.450.0)
- USB Cable (Rs.150.0)
3 affordable item(s)
blocks.explain: Uses enum for categories (Day 20), record for products (Day 20), ArrayList (Day 17), and for-each with if filter (Days 6, 8).

blocks.title: Milestone Exam — Days 16–20
blocks.intro: Exceptions, collections, generics, and modern Java! 70% to pass.

questions.question: Which pair of keywords handles risky code and catches errors?
questions.explain: try runs code that might fail; catch handles the exception. (Day 16)

questions.question: What makes ArrayList different from a plain array?
questions.explain: ArrayList resizes automatically when you add() or remove(). (Day 17)

questions.question: Which collection stores key-value pairs?
questions.explain: HashMap maps each key to a value. (Day 18)

questions.question: What does T in Box<T> represent?
options: A type placeholder you fill in when using the class
questions.explain: T is a generic type parameter — a placeholder for any type. (Day 19)

questions.question: What does a record give you for free?
options: A way to change fields later
questions.explain: A record auto-generates constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString. Immutable. (Day 20)

challenge.title: A robot that knows where it faces
challenge.instructions: Combine both ideas! Make a top-level enum Direction with NORTH, EAST, SOUTH, WEST, and a record Robot(String name, Direction facing). In main, build a robot named Bolt facing NORTH, print it, print which way it faces, and use a switch on r.facing() to print a heading message. Match the expected output exactly.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Robot[name=Bolt, facing=NORTH]
Bolt is facing NORTH
Heading up! ⬆️
challenge.hint: A record can store an enum just like any other type: record Robot(String name, Direction facing) {}. Inside the switch, use the bare constant name (case NORTH ->), not Direction.NORTH.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You combined an enum, a record, and a switch — that's exactly how modern Java models tidy data. Day 20 done!
challenge.failMsg: Make sure Direction has all four constants and Robot is a record with (String name, Direction facing).
